comprehensive_summary:
Anton Chekhov (Anton Pavlovich Chekhov) was a Russian playwright and short story writer, widely considered the master of the short story form. He called medicine his "lawful wife" and literature his "mistress."

"The Lady with the Dog" (or "Lapdog") is Chekhov's most frequently clued story. The unhappily married banker Dmitri Gurov, who originally refers to women as "the lower race," begins an affair with Anna Sergeyevna (wife of the nobleman Von Dideritz) while visiting the resort town of Yalta, where she walks her white Pomeranian. She is startled when Gurov later drops in on her at a performance of The Geisha in her hometown. They reunite and begin a "double life" that "flowed in secret." The story ends with the realization that "the most complicated and difficult part was only just beginning." Gurov eats a watermelon after having sex. Vladimir Nabokov called it "one of the greatest stories ever written." Joyce Carol Oates updated it, setting it in Nantucket.

"Gooseberries" is part of Chekhov's "Little Trilogy" along with "The Man in the Case" and "About Love." In it, Ivan Ivanich (a veterinary surgeon) tells the story of his brother Nikolai, who leaves a government job to buy a farm. Nikolai marries the rich widow of a postmaster and uses her money to buy an estate, where he adopts upper-class mannerisms and gets upset if peasants don't call him "Your Lordship." Nikolai cries the first time he eats fruit from his own bushes. The narrator remarks that "there ought to be someone standing with a little hammer" to remind "every contented, happy man" that unhappy people exist. George Saunders drew the title of A Swim in the Pond in the Rain from this story.

"The Man in the Case" features Byelikov, a teacher of Greek who attempts to hide from the world. He is mocked by a cartoon showing him with a woman (Varinka) captioned "Anthropos in love" and breaks off relations when she laughs after her brother pushes him down stairs. The teacher Burkin narrates the Little Trilogy.

"About Love" features Alehin, who falls in love with his friend Luganovich's wife Anna but they only admit feelings as they are separated forever.

"The Bet" features a lawyer who agrees to be imprisoned for 15 years in exchange for two million rubles. A gun is fired to celebrate his mastery of six languages. The banker plans to kill him near the end. After 15 years, the lawyer kisses the banker on the head, renounces the money, and leaves five hours early, having grown to despise humanity.

"Ward No. 6" draws on Chekhov's medical background. Dr. Andrei Rabin converses with his patient Gromov about Stoic philosophy (Rabin rejects Stoicism). Rabin goes to Warsaw with the postmaster Mikhail to shake off health doubts. Khobotov schemes to consign Rabin to the ward. Rabin is beaten by his caretaker, has a stroke, and is locked in his own asylum. He has a dying vision of a herd of running deer.

"The Darling" features a woman devoted to and who marries three successive men: a theater owner, a lumber merchant, and a veterinarian.

"The Black Monk" features a protagonist named Korvin who is told by the title apparition that he is a genius. He marries the daughter of a horticulturalist.

"Rothschild's Fiddle" (or "Violin"): a coffin-maker nicknamed "Bronze" who plays in a klezmer orchestra overcomes his antisemitism after his wife Marfa dies. The story ends with the title Jew inheriting the violin.

"In the Ravine": a woman goes unpunished after pouring boiling water on Lipa's baby.

"The Student": a clerical apprentice tells the story of Peter's denial of Christ to two widows on Good Friday; Vasilisa is moved to tears.

"Gusev": based on a real burial at sea witnessed during Chekhov's journey to Sakhalin. Virginia Woolf praised it in "Modern Fiction."

Other stories include "Grisha" (a boy gets drunk and wails "Go away, stove!"), "The Lottery Ticket," "Anna on the Neck," and "The Beauties."

The Cherry Orchard is Chekhov's most clued play. Madame Lyubov Ranevskaya sells her estate to Lopakhin, a former serf and businessman. Firs, the old servant, is forgotten and locked in the estate at the end, saying "They've gone away. They've forgotten about me" and lying down to die. A mysterious sound of a breaking string is heard twice -- once in Act II (Firs recalls it was heard when Alexander II freed the serfs) and again at the very end, followed by the distant thud of axes chopping down the title trees. The play is the only Chekhov work where a gun is shown but never fired, breaking his own principle. Ranevskaya painfully recalls the drowning of her son while talking with his former tutor Trofimov. Gayev takes a bank job. A postmaster attends a party and flirts with the maid Dunyasha. Two men kiss at a party where Charlotta does card tricks.

The Seagull features a love triangle between the ingenue Nina Zarechnaya, the playwright Konstantin Treplev, and the popular writer Boris Trigorin. Nina performs Konstantin's experimental symbolist play by a lake, which opens when the moon rises. In it, Nina says "all is cold, void, and terrible" and claims "in me, the consciousness of man has joined hands with the instinct of animal." Satan is represented by two red dots and sulfur fumes. Only Dr. Dorn enjoys the play. Trigorin receives a medallion from Nina inscribed "If you ever need my life, come and take it" (page 121 of his novel). Konstantin shoots the title bird and later shoots himself (twice, the last time fatally). Doctor Dorn claims his medicine bottle made the sound to calm Irina Arkadina. Stanislavski revived the play at the Moscow Art Theatre. Aaron Posner adapted it as Stupid Fucking Bird.

Uncle Vanya: Vanya has spent 25 years tending Professor Serebryakov's estate. In Act III, he tries to shoot Serebryakov but misses, then sinks into a chair. He steals morphine. He exclaims he could have been the next Schopenhauer or Dostoevsky. Dr. Astrov performs a botched surgery while drunk. Helena struggles over the gun with Vanya. The pistol is a classic example of Chekhov's gun.

Three Sisters: about the Prozorov family (Olga, Masha, Irina). Begins on the anniversary of their father's death/Irina's name-day. Baron Tuzenbach is killed in a duel with Soleni over Irina's hand. Chebutykin sings "Ta-ra-ra-boom-di-ay" as Olga says "if only we knew." A military band fades into the distance.

Chekhov's one-act plays include The Bear (Smirnov confesses love for Popova), On the Harmful Effects of Tobacco (Nyukhin gives a boring lecture), The Tragedian in Spite of Himself (Tolkachov rants and screams "I want blood!"), and others.

Chekhov's Gun is his famous dramatic principle: if a gun is shown in Act I, it must be fired by the end. His letters reject labels like "liberal" and "conservative" in favor of the "holy of holies" of the human body and freedom. He wrote the travelogue Sakhalin Island about Siberian penal colonies. Raymond Carver, called the "American Chekhov," depicted his death in the story "Errand." Christopher Durang parodied his works in Vanya and Sonia and Masha and Spike. He lived in the White Dacha in Yalta.

works.name: The Lady with the Dog
works.description: Chekhov's most frequently clued story. Dmitri Gurov begins an affair with Anna Sergeyevna in Yalta, where she walks her Pomeranian. They reunite at a performance of The Geisha. Ends with "the most complicated and difficult part was only just beginning."

clues.clue: Dmitri Gurov begins an affair with Anna Sergeyevna in Yalta; they reunite at a performance of The Geisha
examples: Dmitri Gurov begins an affair with Anna, who is the title 'Lady with the Dog.'
Two characters reunite at a performance of The Geisha after first meeting in Yalta.

clues.clue: Ends with "the most complicated and difficult part was only just beginning"
examples: Another story by this author ends with the realization that 'the hardest, the most complicated part was only just beginning.'
At the end, the central lovers realize that 'the most complicated and difficult part' is 'only just beginning.'

clues.clue: Gurov refers to women as "the lower race"; eats a watermelon after sex; Anna's husband is Von Dideritz
examples: A man who refers to women as 'the lower race.'
A misogynistic man befriends the wife of the nobleman Von Diderits.

clues.clue: They visit Oreanda; she has a white Pomeranian; Anna is called home because her husband has eye problems
examples: Two characters visit the village Oreanda until one of them is called home by her husband because there is something wrong with his eyes.

works.name: The Little Trilogy (Gooseberries / The Man in the Case / About Love)
works.description: Three linked stories narrated by the teacher Burkin. "Gooseberries" is about Nikolai buying a farm; "The Man in the Case" is about the reclusive Byelikov; "About Love" is about Alehin's unrequited love for his friend's wife.

clues.clue: "Gooseberries": Nikolai buys a farm and cries eating his own fruit; "someone standing with a little hammer" to remind happy people of unhappy ones
examples: Nikolai's brother decries him for eating the title fruits.
There ought to be 'someone standing with a little hammer' to remind 'every contented, happy man' that 'there are unhappy people.'

clues.clue: Nikolai marries the rich widow of a postmaster; adopts upper-class mannerisms; gets upset if peasants don't call him "Your Lordship"
examples: A man marries the rich widow of a postmaster, and after she dies, he uses her money to buy an estate.
The narrator's brother becomes upset if the peasants do not refer to him as 'Your Lordship.'

clues.clue: "The Man in the Case": Byelikov, a teacher of Greek, hides from the world; mocked by "Anthropos in love" cartoon; pushed down stairs by Varinka's brother
examples: Byelikov is mocked by a cartoon with the caption 'Anthropos in love.'
A short story about Belikov, a teacher of Greek who attempts to hide himself from the world.

clues.clue: "About Love": Alehin falls in love with his friend Luganovich's wife; they only admit feelings as they are separated forever
examples: Alehin falls in love with the wife of the vice-president of a circuit-court, though they only admit their feelings just before they are separated.

works.name: The Bet
works.description: A lawyer agrees to be imprisoned for 15 years for two million rubles. He masters six languages (celebrated with gunshots), grows to despise humanity, and leaves five hours early, renouncing the money.

clues.clue: A lawyer agrees to 15 years imprisonment for two million rubles; renounces the money and leaves early
examples: A lawyer who argues that life imprisonment and the death penalty are equally immoral renounces two millions after fifteen years.
A man makes the title agreement to stay in jail for fifteen years in exchange for two million rubles.

clues.clue: A gun is fired to celebrate mastery of six languages; the banker kisses the sleeping man on the head
examples: A gun is fired to celebrate a character's mastery of six languages.
The banker kisses a sleeping man on the head after reading about his contempt for earthly life.

works.name: Ward No. 6
works.description: Dr. Andrei Rabin converses with patient Gromov about Stoicism, is locked in his own asylum, beaten by his caretaker, has a stroke, and dies with a vision of running deer.

clues.clue: Dr. Rabin is locked in his own asylum; beaten by caretaker; has a dying vision of running deer
examples: Dr. Andrei Rabin is locked away in his own asylum.
A dying protagonist experiences a vision of running deer.

clues.clue: Rabin converses with patient Gromov about Stoic philosophy; Khobotov schemes to consign him
examples: Ragin argues about Stoic philosophy with Gromov.
Khobotov schemes to consign Dr. Ragin to the title location.

works.name: The Cherry Orchard
works.description: Madame Ranevskaya sells her estate to the former serf Lopakhin. A mysterious breaking string is heard twice. Firs is locked in the estate and lies down to die. The only Chekhov play where a gun appears but is never fired.

clues.clue: Madame Ranevskaya sells her estate to Lopakhin, a former serf/businessman; the title trees are cut down
examples: Lopakhin buys Madame Ranevskaya's estate.
A string breaks as the title property's namesake trees are cut down.

clues.clue: A mysterious breaking string sound is heard twice; followed by the thud of axes at the end
examples: The distant sounds of a string breaking are heard twice.
This sound is followed by the distant thud of axes chopping down trees.

clues.clue: Firs is forgotten and locked in the estate; says "They've gone away. They've forgotten about me" and lies down to die
examples: Servant Firs says 'They've gone away. They've forgotten about me' and lies down to die.

clues.clue: Ranevskaya recalls the drowning of her son; Trofimov is his former tutor
examples: Madame Ranevskaya painfully recalls the drowning of her son while talking with his former tutor Trofimov.

clues.clue: Two men kiss at a party where Charlotta does card tricks; Gayev takes a bank job
examples: Two men kiss at a party at which Charlotta does card tricks.
Gayev takes a job as a bank official.

clues.clue: The only Chekhov play where a gun appears but is never fired
examples: The only play by Chekhov in which a gun is shown but never fired.

works.name: The Seagull
works.description: Love triangle between Nina Zarechnaya, playwright Konstantin Treplev, and writer Boris Trigorin. Konstantin kills the title bird and later shoots himself. Only Dr. Dorn enjoys Konstantin's experimental symbolist play.

clues.clue: Konstantin Treplev writes an experimental symbolist play; only Dr. Dorn enjoys it; Konstantin later shoots himself (twice, fatally)
examples: Nina Zarechnaya performs an experimental symbolist play by Konstantin, who later shoots himself and the title animal.
Only Dr. Dorn enjoys a play written by an author who later shoots himself twice.

clues.clue: Nina's play features "all is cold, void, and terrible"; Satan as two red dots and sulfur fumes; the play takes place by a lake when the moon rises
examples: A character states 'all is cold,' 'void,' and 'terrible.'
Satan is represented by two red dots and fumes of sulfur.

clues.clue: Trigorin receives a medallion from Nina: "If you ever need my life, come and take it" (page 121)
examples: An actress gives her love interest a medallion instructing him to open his most recent novel to page 121.
Trigorin receives a medallion inscribed 'If you ever need my life, come and take it.'

clues.clue: Stanislavski revived the play at the Moscow Art Theatre
examples: Constantin Stanislavski revived the reputation of a play by this man.
The Seagull became popular after its staging at the Moscow Art Theatre.

clues.clue: Aaron Posner adapted it as Stupid Fucking Bird (opens with "Start the fucking play!")
examples: Aaron Posner adapted it as Stupid Fucking Bird, an adaptation of The Seagull.
An adaptation begins when someone yells 'Start the fucking play!'

works.name: Uncle Vanya
works.description: Vanya has spent 25 years tending Professor Serebryakov's estate. He tries to shoot Serebryakov but misses. The pistol is a classic example of Chekhov's gun.

clues.clue: Vanya shoots at and misses Professor Serebryakov in Act III; sinks into a chair; classic example of Chekhov's gun
examples: Vanya angrily shoots at and misses this retired professor.
He sinks into a chair after struggling over the gun with Helena.

clues.clue: Vanya steals morphine; exclaims he could have been Schopenhauer or Dostoevsky
examples: Vanya steals a vial of morphine from a doctor while planning to commit suicide.
Vanya exclaims in despair that he could have been the next of either Schopenhauer or Dostoevsky.

clues.clue: Dr. Astrov performs a botched surgery while drunk; Vanya has tended the estate for 25 years
examples: A doctor performs a botched surgery while drunk.
His anguish stems from the fact that he has been tending a professor's estate for 25 years.

works.name: Three Sisters
works.description: About the Prozorov sisters (Olga, Masha, Irina). Baron Tuzenbach is killed in a duel with Soleni over Irina's hand. Ends with Chebutykin singing "Ta-ra-ra-boom-di-ay" as Olga says "if only we knew."

clues.clue: Baron Tuzenbach killed in a duel with Soleni over Irina's hand; Dr. Chebutykin delivers the news
examples: The Baron was killed in a duel with Soleni over Irina's hand.
A doctor delivers news that the Baron was killed in a duel.

clues.clue: Chebutykin sings "Ta-ra-ra-boom-di-ay"; Olga says "if only we knew"; a military band fades into the distance
examples: This play ends with Chebutykin singing 'Ta-ra-ra-boom-di-ay' as Olga says 'if only we knew.'
A military band fades into the distance.

works.name: Other Stories
works.description: Other frequently referenced stories include "The Black Monk," "Rothschild's Fiddle," "The Darling," "The Student," "In the Ravine," "Gusev," and "Grisha."

clues.clue: "The Black Monk": Korvin is told he is a genius by a phantom monk; marries a horticulturalist's daughter
examples: A 'Master of the Arts' marries the daughter of a horticulturalist after he is told he is a genius by the title figure.
The title figure tells the protagonist he is a genius and can save mankind.

clues.clue: "Rothschild's Fiddle": coffin-maker "Bronze" in klezmer orchestra; overcomes antisemitism after wife Marfa dies; the Jew inherits the violin
examples: A coffin-maker nicknamed 'Bronze' who plays in a klezmer orchestra overcomes his anti-Semitism after his wife Marfa dies.
The title Jew inherits one of the few objects owned by Yakov Ivanov.

clues.clue: "The Student": Peter's denial of Christ told to widows on Good Friday; Vasilisa is moved to tears
examples: A clerical apprentice tells the story of Peter the Apostle's denial of Christ to two widows.
Vasilisa is moved to tears by the story of Peter's denial of Jesus.

clues.clue: "Gusev": based on real burial at sea during Sakhalin journey; praised by Virginia Woolf in "Modern Fiction"
examples: This author's story 'Gusev' is based on a real-life burial at sea that he witnessed during a journey to Sakhalin.
This story is praised in Virginia Woolf's essay 'Modern Fiction.'

clues.clue: "The Darling": a woman devoted to successive husbands -- theater owner, lumber merchant, veterinarian
examples: The title woman is devoted to and marries a theater owner, then a lumber merchant, and then a veterinarian.

clues.clue: "In the Ravine": a woman pours boiling water on Lipa's baby and goes unpunished
examples: A woman goes unpunished after pouring a ladle of boiling water on Lipa's baby.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Russian playwright and master of the short story. Called medicine his "lawful wife" and literature his "mistress." Wrote the travelogue Sakhalin Island. His dramatic principle "Chekhov's gun" states that every element must contribute to the plot.

clues.clue: "Chekhov's gun": if a gun is shown in Act I, it must be fired by the end; a principle of dramatic economy
examples: A principle named for Chekhov claims that if one of these objects is introduced in the first act, it must be used by the last.
A hypothetical gun hung on a wall in the first act names a literary maxim.

clues.clue: Called medicine his "lawful wife" and literature his "mistress"
examples: This author called medicine his 'lawful wife' and literature his 'mistress.'

clues.clue: Raymond Carver ("American Chekhov") depicted his death in the story "Errand"
examples: This author begins bleeding from his mouth in a restaurant and dies in Raymond Carver's story 'Errand.'
Raymond Carver, called the 'American Chekhov,' whose story 'Errand' depicts Chekhov's death.

clues.clue: Wrote the travelogue Sakhalin Island about Siberian penal colonies; lived in the White Dacha in Yalta
examples: This author lamented the overuse of corporal punishment in Siberian penal colonies in his travelogue Sakhalin Island.

clues.clue: Letters reject labels; the "holy of holies" is the human body, health, and freedom
examples: A letter rejects labels like 'liberal' in favor of the 'holy of holies' of the human body, health, and freedom.

clues.clue: Christopher Durang parodied in Vanya and Sonia and Masha and Spike
examples: Christopher Durang's play Vanya and Sonia and Masha and Spike riffs on Uncle Vanya and other plays by this Russian author.

clues.clue: Neil Simon's The Good Doctor is based on his work
examples: This author is the subject of Neil Simon's The Good Doctor.

clues.clue: Ivanov: the title character shoots himself offstage after being challenged by Lvov
examples: This other Chekhov play ends with the title character shooting himself off-stage after being challenged by Lvov.

cards.front: Short Story: Dmitri Gurov begins an affair with Anna Sergeyevna in Yalta; they reunite at a performance of The Geisha in her hometown
Short Story: ends with the realization that "the most complicated and difficult part was only just beginning"
Short Story: a man cries eating fruit from his own bushes; the narrator says "someone with a little hammer" should remind happy people of unhappy ones
cards.work: The Little Trilogy (Gooseberries / The Man in the Case / About Love)

cards.front: Short Story: Byelikov, a teacher of Greek who hides from the world, is mocked by a cartoon captioned "Anthropos in love" and breaks off relations with Varinka
cards.work: The Little Trilogy (Gooseberries / The Man in the Case / About Love)

cards.front: Short Story: a lawyer agrees to 15 years of imprisonment for two million rubles; a gun is fired to celebrate mastery of six languages; he renounces the money and leaves early
Short Story: Dr. Rabin is locked in his own mental asylum after conversing with patient Gromov about Stoicism; has a dying vision of running deer
Play: Madame Ranevskaya sells her estate to the former serf Lopakhin; a mysterious breaking string is heard; Firs is locked in and says "They've forgotten about me"
Play: the only play by this author where a gun is shown but never fired; ends with the sound of axes chopping down the title trees
Play: Konstantin Treplev writes an experimental symbolist play featuring "all is cold, void, and terrible"; kills the title bird and later shoots himself
Play: Trigorin receives a medallion inscribed "If you ever need my life, come and take it" referencing page 121 of his novel
Play: Vanya tries to shoot Professor Serebryakov but misses in Act III; steals morphine; exclaims he could have been Schopenhauer or Dostoevsky
Play: Baron Tuzenbach is killed in a duel with Soleni over Irina's hand; Chebutykin sings "Ta-ra-ra-boom-di-ay" as Olga says "if only we knew"
Short Story: coffin-maker "Bronze" plays in a klezmer orchestra; overcomes antisemitism after wife Marfa dies; the title Jew inherits the violin
Short Story: the title phantom monk tells Korvin he is a genius; appears in a whirl of smoke; Korvin marries a horticulturalist's daughter
Short Story: a clerical apprentice tells widows about Peter's denial of Christ on Good Friday; Vasilisa is moved to tears
Short Story: based on a real burial at sea during a journey to Sakhalin; praised by Virginia Woolf in "Modern Fiction"
Playwright: his dramatic principle states that if a gun is shown in Act I, it must be fired by the end of the play
Playwright: Raymond Carver (the "American Chekhov") depicted this author bleeding from his mouth and dying in the story "Errand"

summary: Anton Chekhov (Anton Pavlovich Chekhov) was a Russian playwright and short story writer, widely considered the master of the short story form.
